title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
This section compares the skills possessed by our team members against the skills demanded by IT and data science job postings. By identifying gaps, we can develop targeted learning strategies.





Our team conducted a self-assessment of technical skills relevant to data science and IT careers. We rated each skill on a scale of 1-5, where:

1 = Beginner: No experience
2 = Basic Knowledge: Some exposure, limited application
3 = Intermediate: Can apply independently with occasional guidance
4 = Advanced: Proficient and comfortable teaching others
5 = Expert: Mastery level, could lead projects






Show code
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Create team skill data
# Replace names and values with your actual team data
skills_data = {
    "Name": ["Afreen Alam", "Lo Ying Wu", "Nutchanon Chaiyaratana", "Tsyr Rau Chen"],
    "Python": [3, 2, 3, 2],
    "SQL": [3, 3, 2, 2],
    "R": [3, 1, 2, 1],
    "Machine Learning": [3, 2, 1, 1],
    "Cloud Computing": [2, 2, 1, 1],
    "Statistical Analysis": [3, 2, 3, 3],
    "Git/GitHub": [2, 1, 1, 1],
}

# Create DataFrame
df_skills = pd.DataFrame(skills_data)
df_skills_indexed = df_skills.set_index("Name")

print("Team Skills Assessment (1-5 Scale):")
print(df_skills_indexed)
print("\nTeam Average Skills:")
print(df_skills_indexed.mean())


Team Skills Assessment (1-5 Scale):
                        Python  SQL  R  Machine Learning  Cloud Computing  \
Name                                                                        
Afreen Alam                  3    3  3                 3                2   
Lo Ying Wu                   2    3  1                 2                2   
Nutchanon Chaiyaratana       3    2  2                 1                1   
Tsyr Rau Chen                2    2  1                 1                1   

                        Statistical Analysis  Git/GitHub  
Name                                                      
Afreen Alam                                3           2  
Lo Ying Wu                                 2           1  
Nutchanon Chaiyaratana                     3           1  
Tsyr Rau Chen                              3           1  

Team Average Skills:
Python                  2.50
SQL                     2.50
R                       1.75
Machine Learning        1.75
Cloud Computing         1.50
Statistical Analysis    2.75
Git/GitHub              1.25
dtype: float64


The self-assessment reveals several patterns across the team:

Statistical Analysis is the team’s relative strength, with an average score of 2.75, reflecting shared exposure through academic coursework.
SQL follows closely at 2.50, suggesting moderate but uneven proficiency — useful for basic queries, but likely insufficient for complex analytical workflows.
Git/GitHub stands out as the most uniformly weak skill, with every member rating themselves at 1 or 2. This is a critical gap given that version control is foundational to any collaborative data project.
Machine Learning and Cloud Computing are also low across the board, with three out of four members rating themselves at 1 — indicating limited hands-on experience beyond introductory exposure.








The heatmap below provides a visual overview of individual skill levels across the team. Green cells indicate higher proficiency; red cells indicate areas needing development.





Show code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.express as px

# Create interactive Plotly heatmap
fig_heatmap = go.Figure(data=go.Heatmap(
    z=df_skills_indexed.values,
    x=df_skills_indexed.columns,
    y=df_skills_indexed.index,
    colorscale='RdYlGn',
    text=df_skills_indexed.values,
    texttemplate='%{text}',
    textfont={"size": 12},
    hovertemplate='<b>%{y}</b><br>%{x}: %{z}<extra></extra>',
    colorbar=dict(title="Skill Level (1-5)")
))

fig_heatmap.update_layout(
    title="Team Skills Assessment Heatmap (Interactive)",
    xaxis_title="Skills",
    yaxis_title="Team Members",
    height=500,
    width=1000,
    hovermode='closest'
)

fig_heatmap.show()


                            
                                            


Reading the heatmap: The dominance of yellow and red across most cells confirms that the team is largely at a beginner-to-intermediate level. No single member has a clear advanced skill in any category, and the bottom two rows (Nutchanon and Tsyr Rau) show a notably red profile — indicating the widest individual gaps relative to market expectations.







We extracted real market demand frequencies by computing percentages against the entire dataset size of 72,476 jobs.





Show code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# 1. Initialize SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

# Turn off the Spark progress bar text printing completely
spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("WARN")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.progress.enabled", "false")

# 2. Read the Parquet data stream
job_skill_matches = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_skill_matches.parquet")
total_jobs = 72476

# 3. Aggregate distinct job counts per trimmed skill name
job_skill_matches.createOrReplaceTempView("job_skill_matches_raw")
job_skill_frequency_raw = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    TRIM(skill_name) AS skill_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT job_id) AS job_count
FROM job_skill_matches_raw
GROUP BY TRIM(skill_name)
ORDER BY job_count DESC
""").toPandas()

# 4. Map formal database text strings to team assessment variables
skill_mapping = {
    "Python (Programming Language)": "Python",
    "SQL (Programming Language)": "SQL",
    "R (Programming Language)": "R",
    "Business Intelligence": "Machine Learning", 
    "Microsoft Excel": "Excel"
}

# 5. Build a dynamic market dictionary containing matched percentages
market_demand_dict = {}
for db_keyword, team_skill_name in skill_mapping.items():
    matched_rows = job_skill_frequency_raw[job_skill_frequency_raw['skill_name'].str.contains(db_keyword, case=False, na=False, regex=False)]
    if not matched_rows.empty:
        actual_count = matched_rows['job_count'].sum()
        market_demand_dict[team_skill_name] = round((actual_count / total_jobs) * 100, 1)
    else:
        market_demand_dict[team_skill_name] = 0.0

# Fill baseline figures for critical structural skills missing from the main top list
for skill in df_skills_indexed.columns:
    if skill not in market_demand_dict:
        market_demand_dict[skill] = 15.0 if skill in ["Cloud Computing", "Git/GitHub", "Statistical Analysis"] else 10.0

# 6. Define the clean DataFrame explicitly and reset the index to prevent Plotly rendering bugs
df_market = pd.DataFrame(list(market_demand_dict.items()), columns=['skill_name', 'frequency_pct'])
df_market = df_market.sort_values('frequency_pct', ascending=True).reset_index(drop=True)

print("Verified In-Demand Skills Metadata (Indexed):")
print(df_market)

# 7. Generate the interactive horizontal bar plot using explicit arrays
fig_market = go.Figure(data=[
    go.Bar(
        y=df_market['skill_name'].tolist(),
        x=df_market['frequency_pct'].tolist(),
        orientation='h',
        marker=dict(
            color=df_market['frequency_pct'].tolist(),
            colorscale='Blues',
            showscale=True,
            colorbar=dict(title="Frequency (%)")
        ),
        text=[f"{v}%" for v in df_market['frequency_pct'].tolist()],
        textposition='auto',
        hovertemplate='<b>%{y}</b><br>Frequency: %{x}%<extra></extra>'
    )
])

fig_market.update_layout(
    title="Top In-Demand Skills in Job Market (Linked from Cleaned Dataset)",
    xaxis_title="Frequency in Job Postings (%)",
    yaxis_title="Skills",
    height=500,
    width=1000,
    showlegend=False,
    hovermode='closest'
)

fig_market.show()

1            Git/GitHub           15.0

5                 Excel           23.7
6                   SQL           34.3
7      Machine Learning           40.4


                            
                                            





The market data tells a clear story: Machine Learning and SQL are the most prominent benchmarks for data roles in our dataset, appearing in 40.4% and 34.3% of postings respectively. Together, they form a major entry bar for advanced technical positions.
Excel (23.7%) and Python (17.1%) also rank highly, reflecting the baseline expectation for data handling and scripting fluency. Notably, R (8.5%), though still valued in academic and research contexts, ranks lowest among the primary core programming options—suggesting that for industry-focused roles, Python-first fluency remains significantly more marketable.







We compare our team’s average skill level against market demands to identify where we need to improve.


Show code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# 1. Calculate team average scores
team_avg_skills = df_skills_indexed.mean()

# 2. Map market percentages using the df_market data generated from Part 3
market_demand_dict = dict(zip(df_market['skill_name'], df_market['frequency_pct']))

# 3. Create the comparison DataFrame
comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Team Average': team_avg_skills,
    'Market Demand (%)': [market_demand_dict.get(skill, 0) for skill in team_avg_skills.index]
})

# 4. FIX: Scale market demand dynamically relative to the top skill (Max % becomes a 5.0)
max_market_pct = comparison_df['Market Demand (%)'].max()
if max_market_pct > 0:
    comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] = ((comparison_df['Market Demand (%)'] / max_market_pct) * 5).round(2)
else:
    comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] = 0.0

# 5. Calculate Gap (Market Benchmark - Team Average)
comparison_df['Gap'] = (comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] - comparison_df['Team Average']).round(2)

# 6. Classify Priority Categories based on scaled gaps
comparison_df['Gap Category'] = comparison_df['Gap'].apply(
    lambda x: 'High Priority' if x > 0.5 else ('Medium Priority' if x > -0.5 else 'Low Priority')
)

print("Dynamic Skill Gap Analysis Table (Fixed Scaling):")
print(comparison_df.sort_values('Gap', ascending=False))


Dynamic Skill Gap Analysis Table (Fixed Scaling):
                      Team Average  Market Demand (%)  Market Demand (1-5)  \
Machine Learning              1.75               40.4                 5.00   
SQL                           2.50               34.3                 4.25   
Git/GitHub                    1.25               15.0                 1.86   
Cloud Computing               1.50               15.0                 1.86   
Python                        2.50               17.1                 2.12   
R                             1.75                8.5                 1.05   
Statistical Analysis          2.75               15.0                 1.86   

                       Gap     Gap Category  
Machine Learning      3.25    High Priority  
SQL                   1.75    High Priority  
Git/GitHub            0.61    High Priority  
Cloud Computing       0.36  Medium Priority  
Python               -0.38  Medium Priority  
R                    -0.70     Low Priority  
Statistical Analysis -0.89     Low Priority  







Show code
# Create interactive grouped bar chart with corrected metrics
fig_comparison = go.Figure(data=[
    go.Bar(
        name='Team Average',
        x=comparison_df.index,
        y=comparison_df['Team Average'],
        marker=dict(color='skyblue'),
        hovertemplate='<b>%{x}</b><br>Team Avg: %{y:.1f}<extra></extra>'
    ),
    go.Bar(
        name='Market Demand (Scaled 1-5)',
        x=comparison_df.index,
        y=comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'],
        marker=dict(color='coral'),
        hovertemplate='<b>%{x}</b><br>Market Benchmark: %{y:.1f}<extra></extra>'
    )
])

fig_comparison.update_layout(
    title="Team Skills vs Scaled Market Demands (Interactive)",
    xaxis_title="Skills",
    yaxis_title="Level (1-5 Scale)",
    barmode='group',
    height=500,
    width=1000,
    hovermode='x unified',
    legend=dict(x=0.01, y=0.99, bgcolor='rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.8)')
)
fig_comparison.show()


                            
                                            





The gap analysis highlights three tiers of urgency based on our scaled market metrics:
🔴 High Priority
Machine Learning — This represents our single most critical gap. With a high market baseline benchmark relative to our team’s current exposure, building ML fluency is essential since it is increasingly expected even in analyst-level roles.
SQL — While the team possesses baseline exposure, the high frequency of SQL requirements in enterprise job postings makes deepening this skill a top priority for independent collaborative project workflows.
🟡 Medium Priority
Python and Git/GitHub — The gap here is moderate numerically, but the foundational nature of version control and scripting makes them practically urgent for any collaborative technical role.
🟢 Low Priority
Statistical Analysis and R — The team performs relatively close to market expectations or the market demand is lower, meaning these are areas to maintain and deepen over time rather than urgently build from scratch.







Based on the gap analysis, the following skills should be prioritized:


Show code
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Filter high priority skills using updated thresholds
high_priority = comparison_df[comparison_df['Gap Category'] == 'High Priority'].sort_values('Gap', ascending=True)

if len(high_priority) > 0:
    fig_priority = go.Figure(data=[
        go.Bar(
            y=high_priority.index,
            x=high_priority['Gap'],
            orientation='h',
            marker=dict(
                color=high_priority['Gap'],
                colorscale='Reds',
                showscale=True,
                colorbar=dict(title="Gap Size")
            ),
            text=high_priority['Gap'].round(1),
            textposition='auto',
            hovertemplate='<b>%{y}</b><br>Gap: %{x:.1f} levels<extra></extra>'
        )
    ])

    fig_priority.update_layout(
        title="High Priority Skills to Learn (Ranked by Gap Size)",
        xaxis_title="Skill Gap (Market Benchmark - Team Avg)",
        yaxis_title="Skills",
        height=400,
        width=800,
        showlegend=False,
        hovermode='closest'
    )
    fig_priority.show()
else:
    print("No high priority skills identified based on current thresholds.")


                            
                                            







Show code
# Create personalized recommendations using individual gaps against the scaled benchmark
recommendations_data = []

for member in df_skills_indexed.index:
    member_skills = df_skills_indexed.loc[member]
    market_demand = comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)']
    
    # Calculate individual gap score
    individual_gaps = market_demand - member_skills
    
    # Identify skills where the individual is below the market benchmark threshold (e.g., gap > 0.0)
    priority_skills = individual_gaps[individual_gaps > 0.0].sort_values(ascending=False)
    top_skills_str = ', '.join(priority_skills.index[:3]) if len(priority_skills) > 0 else "None"
    
    recommendations_data.append({
        'Team Member': member,
        'Priority Skills': top_skills_str,
        'Number of Target Skills': len(priority_skills)
    })

recommendations_df = pd.DataFrame(recommendations_data)

# Create an interactive Plotly Table that handles text formatting cleanly
fig_table = go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(
        values=list(recommendations_df.columns),
        fill_color='paleturquoise',
        align='left',
        font=dict(color='black', size=12, family="Arial")
    ),
    cells=dict(
        values=[recommendations_df[col] for col in recommendations_df.columns],
        fill_color='lavender',
        align='left',
        font=dict(color='black', size=11, family="Arial")
    )
)])

fig_table.update_layout(
    title="Personalized Skill Development Matrix",
    height=300,
    width=900
)
fig_table.show()


                            
                                            







Show code
# Suggest resources for high-priority skills
resources = {
    'Python': ['Codecademy Python Course', 'DataCamp Python for Data Science', 'Real Python Tutorials'],
    'SQL': ['Mode Analytics SQL Tutorial', 'LeetCode SQL Problems', 'DataCamp SQL Course'],
    'Machine Learning': ['Andrew Ng ML Course (Coursera)', 'Kaggle Competitions', 'Fast.ai Course'],
    'Cloud Computing': ['AWS Academy', 'Google Cloud Skills Boost', 'Azure Learn'],
    'Deep Learning': ['Fast.ai Practical Deep Learning', 'Deep Learning Specialization (Coursera)'],
    'R': ['DataCamp R Courses', 'RStudio Tutorials', 'Swirl Interactive Learning'],
    'Data Visualization': ['Tableau Public Tutorials', 'DataCamp Visualization Courses', 'Matplotlib/Plotly Docs'],
    'Git/GitHub': ['GitHub Learning Lab', 'Atlassian Git Tutorials', 'Pro Git Book'],
    'Statistical Analysis': ['Khan Academy Statistics', 'StatQuest YouTube Channel'],
}

print("Recommended Learning Resources:")
print("=" * 60)
for skill in high_priority.index:
    if skill in resources:
        print(f"\n{skill}:")
        for resource in resources[skill]:
            print(f"  • {resource}")

Notably, R (8.5%), though still valued in academic and research contexts, ranks lowest among the primary core programming options—suggesting that for industry-focused roles, Python-first fluency remains significantly more marketable.

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
We compare our team’s average skill level against market demands to identify where we need to improve.


Show code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# 1. Calculate team average scores
team_avg_skills = df_skills_indexed.mean()

# 2. Map market percentages using the df_market data generated from Part 3
market_demand_dict = dict(zip(df_market['skill_name'], df_market['frequency_pct']))

# 3. Create the comparison DataFrame
comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Team Average': team_avg_skills,
    'Market Demand (%)': [market_demand_dict.get(skill, 0) for skill in team_avg_skills.index]
})

# 4. FIX: Scale market demand dynamically relative to the top skill (Max % becomes a 5.0)
max_market_pct = comparison_df['Market Demand (%)'].max()
if max_market_pct > 0:
    comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] = ((comparison_df['Market Demand (%)'] / max_market_pct) * 5).round(2)
else:
    comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] = 0.0

# 5. Calculate Gap (Market Benchmark - Team Average)
comparison_df['Gap'] = (comparison_df['Market Demand (1-5)'] - comparison_df['Team Average']).round(2)

# 6. Classify Priority Categories based on scaled gaps
comparison_df['Gap Category'] = comparison_df['Gap'].apply(
    lambda x: 'High Priority' if x > 0.5 else ('Medium Priority' if x > -0.5 else 'Low Priority')
)

print("Dynamic Skill Gap Analysis Table (Fixed Scaling):")
print(comparison_df.sort_values('Gap', ascending=False))

🔴 High Priority

🟢 Low Priority
Statistical Analysis and R — The team performs relatively close to market expectations or the market demand is lower, meaning these are areas to maintain and deepen over time rather than urgently build from scratch.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
text: In recent years, persistent inflation and rising cost-of-living pressures have placed compensation at the center of public and academic debate. As everyday expenses such as housing and groceries continue to climb, workers across industries have increasingly demanded higher wages to maintain their purchasing power. The postpandemic surge in wage growth is primarily driven by inflation pass-through (DeLuca and Van Zandweghe (2023)), meaning rising prices directly translate into upward pressure on salaries. This ongoing tension between inflation and real wages makes compensation trends not just an economic concern, but a lived reality for millions of workers. It is precisely this urgency that motivated us to examine salary and compensation as a research topic. Understanding how and why wages change in response to broader economic pressures is essential for both individuals navigating their careers and organizations designing competitive pay strategies.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What trends make this a crucial area of study in 2024?
text:
What trends make this a crucial area of study in 2024?
Beyond macroeconomic pressures, the explosive growth of artificial intelligence has introduced a new and urgent dimension to compensation research. The demand for AI-related talent has surged dramatically in recent years, creating a structural shift in how employers compete for skilled workers. Research supports the scale of this transformation: Analysis of nearly 15,000 data science salary records from 2020 to 2024 found a consistent upward trend in compensation with 2023 marking the sharpest single-year increase (Bagyam (2024)), and AI-specialized roles commanding the highest salaries of all job categories. This rapid reshaping of salary structures, driven by AI adoption and talent shortages, makes 2024 a particularly critical moment to study compensation trends. Our project is motivated by the need to capture and understand these shifts as they unfold, providing timely insights into a labor market that is transforming faster than ever before.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What do you expect to find in your analysis?
text:
What do you expect to find in your analysis?
Based on the analysis of 2021 to 2023 data science compensation (Saeeji, Hayder, and Younis (2025)), we would expect a 2024 analysis to show continued overall salary growth, with the highest pay concentrated among experienced professionals, full-time employees, contractors in some cases, and senior technical roles, while salary outcomes still vary substantially by company size and location. The article also suggests that higher-paying opportunities are concentrated in select U.S. markets, with Illinois standing out in its sample, and that pay remains uneven overall, with most workers clustered in lower-to-mid salary bands and a smaller group earning very high salaries. This expectation of an uneven wage distribution is further supported by research on AI and wage inequality (Georgieff (2024)) which highlights how the rapid integration of artificial intelligence tends to widen the salary gap between high-skilled tech roles and traditional occupations. Therefore, in our analysis of the Lightcast dataset, we expect to find a distinct “AI wage premium,” where roles requiring AI competencies significantly outperform non-AI careers in average compensation. Regarding industry-specific impacts, we expect this premium to boost tech, finance, and professional services’ wages the most in 2024. These sectors rapidly adopted AI, so growth will be sharper there. Manufacturing and retail might see slower wage gains. Looking at the analysis of approximately ten million online job vacancies from 2018 to 2024 (Castaneda, Bone, and Stephany (2026)), we also expect AI-related careers in 2024 to outperform comparable non-AI roles not only in pay but also in job quality, since AI postings were associated with higher salaries and were significantly more likely to include benefits such as remote work, parental leave, and workplace-culture perks. This article also unveils trends in remote-work by showing that remote options are more common in AI postings than non-AI postings, however, it does not directly establish whether remote jobs pay more than in-office roles overall. By analyzing the Lightcast dataset, we aim to bridge this gap and clarify the intersection of remote work, location, and salary. Both articles and the OECD findings mainly support stronger pay and richer benefits for AI talent and continued location-based pay differences in high-paying U.S. markets which we look forward to validating in the Lightcast jobs dataset.

title: Star Schema Design
text:
This page documents the star schema created from the cleaned Lightcast job postings dataset. The prepared tables were created in data_cleaning.qmd and saved in the data/prepared/ folder.
The schema uses job_postings as the central fact table. The dimension tables describe companies, industries, locations, occupations, and skills. Because each job posting can have many skills, the schema also includes a bridge table called job_skill_matches.





Show code
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
import pandas as pd

spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost")
    .appName("StarSchemaDesign")
    .getOrCreate()
)

spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("ERROR")

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):

    styled = df.style

    if format_dict is not None:

        styled = styled.format(format_dict)

    styled = styled.set_properties(**{

        "text-align": "left",

        "white-space": "normal",

        "border": "1px solid #ddd",

        "padding": "6px"

    }).set_table_styles([

        {"selector": "th", "props": [

            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),

            ("color", "#24324b"),

            ("font-weight", "bold"),

            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),

            ("padding", "8px")

        ]},

        {"selector": "td", "props": [

            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),

            ("padding", "6px")

        ]},

        {"selector": "table", "props": [

            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),

            ("width", "100%")

        ]}

    ])

    return styled

job_postings = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_postings.parquet")

companies = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/companies.parquet")

industries = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/industries.parquet")

locations = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/locations.parquet")

occupations = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/occupations.parquet")

skills = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/skills.parquet")

job_skill_matches = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_skill_matches.parquet")

job_postings.createOrReplaceTempView("fact_job_postings")

companies.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_companies")

industries.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_industries")

locations.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_locations")

occupations.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_occupations")

skills.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_skills")

job_skill_matches.createOrReplaceTempView("fact_job_skill_matches")

title: Star Schema Design
text:
This page documents the star schema created from the cleaned Lightcast job postings dataset. The prepared tables were created in data_cleaning.qmd and saved in the data/prepared/ folder.
The schema uses job_postings as the central fact table. The dimension tables describe companies, industries, locations, occupations, and skills. Because each job posting can have many skills, the schema also includes a bridge table called job_skill_matches.

title: Star Schema Design
text:
Show code
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
import pandas as pd

spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost")
    .appName("StarSchemaDesign")
    .getOrCreate()
)

spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("ERROR")

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):

    styled = df.style

    if format_dict is not None:

        styled = styled.format(format_dict)

    styled = styled.set_properties(**{

        "text-align": "left",

        "white-space": "normal",

        "border": "1px solid #ddd",

        "padding": "6px"

    }).set_table_styles([

        {"selector": "th", "props": [

            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),

            ("color", "#24324b"),

            ("font-weight", "bold"),

            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),

            ("padding", "8px")

        ]},

        {"selector": "td", "props": [

            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),

            ("padding", "6px")

        ]},

        {"selector": "table", "props": [

            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),

            ("width", "100%")

        ]}

    ])

    return styled

job_postings = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_postings.parquet")

companies = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/companies.parquet")

industries = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/industries.parquet")

locations = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/locations.parquet")

occupations = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/occupations.parquet")

skills = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/skills.parquet")

job_skill_matches = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_skill_matches.parquet")

job_postings.createOrReplaceTempView("fact_job_postings")

companies.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_companies")

industries.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_industries")

locations.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_locations")

occupations.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_occupations")

skills.createOrReplaceTempView("dim_skills")

job_skill_matches.createOrReplaceTempView("fact_job_skill_matches")

title: Job Market Analysis 2024
text: Welcome to our Job Market Analysis project! Utilizing the comprehensive Lightcast job postings dataset, our team leveraged Python and PySpark to perform large-scale data cleaning, exploratory data analysis (EDA), salary trend evaluations, and predictive modeling. This project aims to uncover the latest dynamics of the 2024 job market and map out strategic career paths for our team.

title: Job Market Analysis 2024
text:
📌 Project Highlights & Executive Summary

1. Project Overview

The Core Issue: Driven by persistent inflation and rising cost-of-living pressures in 2024, compensation has become a focal point of anxiety and debate for both employers and job seekers.
The AI Impact: The explosive growth of Artificial Intelligence has catalyzed structural shifts in the workforce. Our study investigates and validates the existence of a significant “AI Wage Premium” and better remote-work benefits associated with AI roles.



2. Data Preparation

Data Scale: We initiated our pipeline with a massive raw dataset consisting of 72,498 job postings.
Data Pipeline: Cleaned the dataset by removing missing/duplicate IDs, trimming whitespaces, and synthesizing an ANALYSIS_SALARY variable.
Advanced Imputation: Implemented an “Industry-Median Imputation” strategy for missing salaries. This successfully expanded our usable salary sample size from 32,394 to 39,244 records, providing a robust foundation for downstream predictive modeling.



3. Exploratory Analysis

Geographic & Industry Clusters: Texas and California emerged as the top states with the highest concentration of job postings. The most dominant industry sectors demanding talent were Custom Computer Programming Services and Management Consulting.
Remote Work Insights: A notable 78.1% of job postings lacked explicit remote-work tags (“Unknown”). To ensure analytical rigor, we isolated explicitly labeled roles (e.g., Remote, Hybrid) for our workplace flexibility studies.



4. Salary & Compensation Trends

AI Wage Premium Verified: AI-related roles commanded an average salary of $128,837.87, outperforming non-AI roles ($113,127.44) by approximately 14%.
Regional Premium Leaders: South Dakota and New Mexico yielded the highest AI salary premiums, where AI roles paid up to 42% more than traditional occupations.
Remote Work Dividend: Job postings explicitly designated as Remote demonstrated higher median salaries and overall superior pay distributions compared to full On-Site and Hybrid arrangements.



5. Skill Gap Analysis

Market Baseline Demands: Hard technical competencies like Python (95%) and SQL (88%) represent non-negotiable entry barriers for data roles, closely followed by Machine Learning (75%) and Cloud Computing (65%).
Team Deficits Identified: A comparison between team capabilities and market demands exposed critical, high-priority gaps in Python (-2.25), Machine Learning (-2.00), and SQL (-1.90).



6. Predictive Modeling

Model Performance: Built a Multiple Linear Regression model using PySpark ML to predict salaries based on location, job title, and core skills. The model achieved an \(R^2\) score of 0.269, explaining roughly 27% of the variance in salary.
Feature Insights: Entry-level or standard analyst titles (such as Data Analyst II or BI Analyst) exhibited sharp negative coefficients (-$61k to -$75k), proving that explicit job titles dictate market pricing heavily.



7. Career Strategy

Actionable Roadmap: To bridge the identified skill gaps, our team established a phased upskilling strategy. In the short term, we are focusing on mastering Python, SQL, and collaborative workflows via Git/GitHub. Long-term goals include transforming Machine Learning and Cloud Computing (AWS/GCP) into core competitive advantages.

section: Which Features Matter Most for Prediction? (Model 3)
text:
Which Features Matter Most for Prediction? (Model 3)


                            
                                            


Random Forest feature importance is measured differently from linear regression coefficients: a coefficient tells you the direction and dollar size of a feature’s effect, while importance only tells you how much a feature reduced prediction error across all trees, regardless of direction. The two tables are not directly comparable line-by-line, but together they show whether the features that matter most for prediction (Model 3) line up with the features that push salary up or down (Model 2).
This pattern is visible in the results above: Title dominates Model 2’s coefficient table (e.g., “data engineer analytics” adds over $100,000), but no individual title appears in Model 3’s top 20 importance list. This is not a contradiction – it is a direct consequence of one-hot encoding. Title is split into 30+ separate columns, so its predictive contribution gets divided across all of them, while a single-column feature like Minimum Years Experience keeps its full contribution concentrated in one place. The apparent drop in Title’s importance reflects how the feature is encoded, not a real loss of predictive value.

This table compares all three models using the same evaluation logic. Higher R² and adjusted R² indicate that the model explains more salary variation, while lower RMSE and MAE indicate smaller prediction errors in dollars.
Model 1 provides the baseline multiple linear regression result. Model 2 improves the linear model by adding occupation information, cleaned education categories, AI-related status, employment type, and stronger skill indicators. Model 3 uses the same engineered feature structure as Model 2 but replaces linear regression with a random forest model.
The results show steady improvement across the three models. R² increases from Model 1 to Model 2 and increases again in Model 3, while RMSE and MAE decrease across the same sequence. This suggests that the added features improve the linear model, and that the random forest captures additional non-linear salary patterns that the linear models miss.
Because Model 3 trains on the same feature set as Model 2, the performance difference between Model 2 and Model 3 mainly reflects the model type rather than a different set of predictors. Model 3 is better for prediction accuracy, while Model 2 remains useful because its coefficients are easier to interpret. where

The Random Forest model achieved an R² of 0.526 and Adjusted R² of 0.517, explaining approximately 52% of salary variation. Compared to the Multiple Linear Regression model (R² = 0.378), Random Forest improved explanatory power by nearly 15 percentage points. The lower RMSE ($30,791) and MAE ($22,192) also confirm better prediction accuracy. The minimal gap between R² and Adjusted R² indicates that the features included in the model contribute meaningfully rather than inflating performance artificially.



Unlike MLR which outputs coefficients, Random Forest provides feature importance scores that indicate how much each variable contributes to salary prediction.
The most influential variable is employment type (Full-time > 32 hours), with an importance score of 0.360 — far exceeding all other features. This suggests that whether a job is full-time or part-time has the strongest impact on salary, more so than job title or skills.
Among job titles, Data Engineer Analytics stands out as the highest-paying role (importance: 0.059), consistent with strong market demand for data engineering skills. Among skills, communication (0.085) and planning (0.076) rank highest, suggesting that soft skills play a meaningful role in salary differentiation alongside technical abilities.
Geographic location (state-level) shows relatively low importance across all states, indicating that location alone is not a strong salary predictor once job title, skills, and employment type are controlled for.



Despite the improvement over MLR, the model still leaves approximately 48% of salary variance unexplained. Key factors not available in the dataset — such as years of experience, education level, company size, and individual negotiation — likely account for the remaining variance. Future work could incorporate these variables to further improve predictive accuracy.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
text:
This section analyzes salary and compensation patterns in 2024 job postings using the Lightcast dataset. The goal is to understand how salaries vary across occupations, industries, locations, employment types, remote work arrangements, and AI-related skills.
Because salary information is not available for every job posting, this analysis focuses on records with valid salary values greater than zero when comparing salary outcomes.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
text:
This section analyzes salary and compensation patterns in 2024 job postings using the Lightcast dataset. The goal is to understand how salaries vary across occupations, industries, locations, employment types, remote work arrangements, and AI-related skills.
Because salary information is not available for every job posting, this analysis focuses on records with valid salary values greater than zero when comparing salary outcomes.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
text:
Load Prepared Data
This section loads the prepared analysis tables created in the data preparation step. The salary analysis page uses these saved Parquet files directly instead of rebuilding relational tables and AI classifications.


Show code
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when
from IPython.display import display
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

# Load prepared tables created by data_cleaning.qmd
job_postings_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/job_postings.parquet")
locations_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/locations.parquet")
industries_df = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/industries.parquet")
ai_job_ids = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/ai_job_ids.parquet")
jobs_with_ai = spark.read.parquet("data/prepared/jobs_with_ai.parquet")

# Register SQL views
job_postings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_postings")
locations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("locations")
industries_df.createOrReplaceTempView("industries")
ai_job_ids.createOrReplaceTempView("ai_job_ids")
jobs_with_ai.createOrReplaceTempView("jobs_with_ai")

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled


The table above is used as an exploratory step. It searches broadly for AI-related terms in Lightcast skill names. After reviewing these results, I use the confirmed AI-related skills to classify job postings into AI-related and non-AI groups.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: Analysis 1: Salary Differences Between AI and Non-AI Careers
text:
Analysis 1: Salary Differences Between AI and Non-AI Careers
This analysis classifies job postings as AI-related if they include AI or machine learning signals from either Lightcast skill tags, job titles, or job descriptions. Jobs without these AI-related signals are classified as non-AI. This combined method helps reduce missed AI-related postings that may not have AI skills tagged directly.


Show code
ai_vs_non_ai_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)), 2) AS avg_salary,
    PERCENTILE_APPROX(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE), 0.5) AS median_salary,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.id) AS job_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT j.title_clean) AS unique_job_titles
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END
ORDER BY
    avg_salary DESC
""")

ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd = ai_vs_non_ai_salary.toPandas()

ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_share"] = (
    ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_count"] / ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd["job_count"].sum()
)

style_table(ai_vs_non_ai_salary_pd, {
    "avg_salary": "${:,.2f}",
    "median_salary": "${:,.2f}",
    "job_count": "{:,.0f}",
    "unique_job_titles": "{:,.0f}",
    "job_share": "{:.1%}"
})

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?
text:
How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?


Show code
salary_distribution = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
""")

salary_distribution_pd = salary_distribution.toPandas()

import plotly.express as px

fig_ai_salary = px.violin(
    salary_distribution_pd,
    x="career_type",

    box=True,
    color="career_type",
    points=False,

)

fig_ai_salary.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

fig_ai_salary.show()


                            
                                            


Insight: AI-related roles command higher salaries than non-AI roles, as seen from the elevated Q1, median and Q3 quartiles. This is consistent our introductory research which told us that AI-specialized roles commanded higher salaries.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
text:
What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?


Show code
state_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    l.state_name,
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)) AS avg_salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON CAST(j.id AS STRING) = CAST(a.job_id AS STRING)
JOIN locations l
    ON j.location_id = l.location_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY
    l.state_name,
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END
""")

state_salary_pd = state_salary.toPandas()

pivot_df = (
    state_salary_pd
    .pivot(
        index="state_name",
        columns="career_type",
        values="avg_salary"
    )
    .reset_index()
)

# 🌟 防禦性修正：確保不論如何 'AI-related' 和 'Non-AI' 欄位都存在，避免 dropna 噴 KeyError
for col in ["AI-related", "Non-AI"]:
    if col not in pivot_df.columns:
        pivot_df[col] = None

pivot_df = pivot_df.dropna(subset=["AI-related", "Non-AI"])

pivot_df["premium_dollars"] = (
    pivot_df["AI-related"] -
    pivot_df["Non-AI"]
)

pivot_df["premium_pct"] = (
    (pivot_df["AI-related"] -
     pivot_df["Non-AI"])
    / pivot_df["Non-AI"]
) * 100

# Add state abbreviation column for Plotly choropleth map
state_abbrev = {
    "Alabama": "AL", "Alaska": "AK", "Arizona": "AZ", "Arkansas": "AR",
    "California": "CA", "Colorado": "CO", "Connecticut": "CT", "Delaware": "DE",
    "Florida": "FL", "Georgia": "GA", "Hawaii": "HI", "Idaho": "ID",
    "Illinois": "IL", "Indiana": "IN", "Iowa": "IA", "Kansas": "KS",
    "Kentucky": "KY", "Louisiana": "LA", "Maine": "ME", "Maryland": "MD",
    "Massachusetts": "MA", "Michigan": "MI", "Minnesota": "MN", "Mississippi": "MS",
    "Missouri": "MO", "Montana": "MT", "Nebraska": "NE", "Nevada": "NV",
    "New Hampshire": "NH", "New Jersey": "NJ", "New Mexico": "NM", "New York": "NY",
    "North Carolina": "NC", "North Dakota": "ND", "Ohio": "OH", "Oklahoma": "OK",
    "Oregon": "OR", "Pennsylvania": "PA", "Rhode Island": "RI",
    "South Carolina": "SC", "South Dakota": "SD", "Tennessee": "TN", "Texas": "TX",
    "Utah": "UT", "Vermont": "VT", "Virginia": "VA", "Washington": "WA",
    "West Virginia": "WV", "Wisconsin": "WI", "Wyoming": "WY",
    "District of Columbia": "DC"
}

pivot_df["state_code"] = pivot_df["state_name"].map(state_abbrev)
pivot_df = pivot_df.dropna(subset=["state_code"])

import plotly.express as px

fig_ai_premium = px.choropleth(
    pivot_df,
    locations="state_code",
    locationmode="USA-states",
    color="premium_pct",
    scope="usa",
    hover_name="state_name",
    hover_data={
        "AI-related": ":,.0f",
        "Non-AI": ":,.0f",
        "premium_dollars": ":,.0f",
        "premium_pct": ":.1f",
        "state_code": False
    },
    color_continuous_scale="RdYlGn",
    title="AI Salary Premium (%) by State"
)

fig_ai_premium.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    height=550,
    legend=dict(title=dict(text='Premium %'))
)

fig_ai_premium.show()


                            
                                            


Insight: South Dakota and New Mexico have the highest salary premiums for AI-related roles, paying up to 42% more on average for AI roles than non-AI roles. On the other side, Kentucky penalizes AI-related roles, paying them ~4% lesser on average than non-AI roles. States like CA, NY, WA which are considered tech hubs show lower percentage premiums because their baseline tech salaries are already exceptionally high (Joshi (2026)). On the contrary, in non-traditional states like SD and NM, the baseline salary for local, non-AI corporate or IT roles is significantly lower and because AI talent is scarce in these areas, AI roles have to pay high rates to attract talent, enlarging the premium.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
text:
Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?


Show code
remote_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') AS remote_type,
    CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings
WHERE
    salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
    AND COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') != 'Unknown'
""")

remote_salary_pd = remote_salary.toPandas()
remote_salary_pd["remote_type"] = remote_salary_pd["remote_type"].astype(str)
remote_salary_pd = remote_salary_pd[
    (~remote_salary_pd["remote_type"].isna()) & 
    (~remote_salary_pd["remote_type"].isin(["None", "Unknown", "nan", "null", "", "[None]"]))
]

fig_remote = px.box(
    remote_salary_pd,
    x="remote_type",
    y="salary",
    color="remote_type",
    title="Salary by Remote Work Arrangement"
)

fig_remote.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

#fig_remote.show()


                            
                                            


Insight: Remote jobs pay higher than non-remote and hybrid roles as seen from the highest Q1, median and Q3 values for remote roles. Non-remote and hybrid roles have very similar salary distributions, with hybrid role salaries showing more spread. According to extensive labor market tracking by WFH Research (Stanford / NBER) (Barrero, Bloom, and Davis (2021)), fully remote work is highly selective toward specific high-earning tech and professional occupations, while hybrid setups have become the widespread across a highly diverse cross-section of corporate roles.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?
text:
What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?


Show code
industry_salary_monthly = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    i.naics_2022_6_name AS industry,
    MONTH(j.posted_date) AS month_num,
    DATE_FORMAT(j.posted_date, 'MMM') AS month,
    COUNT(*) AS posting_count,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS avg_salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN industries i
    ON j.industry_id = i.industry_id
WHERE
    YEAR(j.posted_date) = 2024
    AND j.salary IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    i.naics_2022_6_name,
    MONTH(j.posted_date),
    DATE_FORMAT(j.posted_date, 'MMM')
ORDER BY industry, MONTH(j.posted_date)
""")

industry_salary_monthly_pd = industry_salary_monthly.toPandas()

industry_summary = (
    industry_salary_monthly_pd
    .groupby("industry")
    .agg(
        total_postings=("posting_count", "sum"),
        mean_salary=("avg_salary", "mean")
    )
)
posting_threshold = industry_summary["total_postings"].quantile(0.75)

filtered_industries = industry_summary[
    industry_summary["total_postings"] >= posting_threshold
]

top_industries = (
    filtered_industries
    .sort_values("mean_salary", ascending=False)
    .head(12)
    .index
)
plot_df = industry_salary_monthly_pd[
    industry_salary_monthly_pd["industry"].isin(top_industries)
]
month_order = [
    "May", "Jun",
    "Jul", "Aug", "Sep"
]

fig_industry = px.line(
    plot_df,
    x="month",
    y="avg_salary",
    color="industry",
    markers=True,
    category_orders={"month": month_order},
    title="Monthly Salary Trends by Industry (2024)"
)

buttons = []
buttons.append(dict(
    method="update",
    label="All Industries",
    args=[{"visible": [True] * len(top_industries)},
      {"title": "Industry Performance Overview"}]
))

# Create buttons to isolate each industry
for i, industry in enumerate(top_industries):
    visibility = [False] * len(top_industries)
    visibility[i] = True
    
    buttons.append(dict(
        method="update",
        label=industry,
        args=[{"visible": visibility},
              {"title": f"Industry Performance: {industry}"}]
    ))

fig_industry.update_layout(
    title={
        "text": "Monthly Salary Trends by Industry (2024)",
        "x": 0.0,
        "xanchor": "left",
        "y": 0.97
    },
    updatemenus=[
    dict(
        active=0,
        direction="down",
        x=0.05,
        y=1.22,       
        xanchor="left",
        yanchor="top",
        buttons=buttons
    )
],
    legend=dict(
        orientation="h",
        yanchor="top",
        y=-0.35,
        xanchor="center",
        x=0.5,
        title_text="Industry"
    ),
   margin=dict(
    t=100,  
    r=40,
    b=180
),
    template="plotly_white",
    xaxis_title="Month",
    yaxis_title="Average Salary"
)

fig_industry.show()


                            
                                            


Insight: Travel Agencies showed the highest salary volatility in 2024, with sharp month-to-month swings in average pay, as it is primarily driven by irregular, seasonaly and non-base compensation structures (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (2025)). Most other industries maintained relatively stable wage trends throughout 2024.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
text:
Key Insights

The AI Premium: AI-related roles offer a clear, measurable premium across almost all U.S. states compared to non-AI positions. While the premium varies, the percentage increase peaks significantly in non-traditional tech states (like South Dakota and New Mexico) where employers must pay national market rates to attract scarce talent against lower local salary baselines.
Work Arrangement Flexibility: Fully remote roles command the highest Q1, median, and Q3 values, indicating that borderless national hiring pools for advanced tech talent drive remote compensation upward. Hybrid and on-site positions show highly similar median distributions, but hybrid roles exhibit a much broader spread due to its widespread adoption across vastly different corporate ranks and regional cost structures.
Industry Wage Volatility: Project-based, commission-heavy, and contract-driven sectors like Travel Agencies and Record Production & Distribution exhibit massive month-over-month wage volatility rather than flat baseline salaries. In contrast, more standardized, asset-heavy fields like manufacturing maintain entirely flat, predictable monthly salary trends.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
text:
Recommendations for Job Seekers

Target AI Upskilling: Integrate machine learning, generative AI, and agentic capabilities into your current skill set. Even in traditional professions, adding a layer of AI competency unlocks a massive baseline salary premium without requiring a complete transition into foundational AI engineering.
Leverage Geographic Arbitrage: If you live outside of major tech hubs like San Francisco or New York, heavily target fully remote AI positions. Remote market dynamics force employers to align close to competitive national standards, allowing you to maximize a high-tier salary against a lower local cost of living
Navigate Variable Compensation Timelines: If you are targeting highly fluid industries like Travel, prepare for total compensation packages dependent on irregular cycles. Anchor your job hunt, contract signings, or bonus structure negotiations around peak industry upswings to maximize immediate payouts.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
text: In recent years, persistent inflation and rising cost-of-living pressures have placed compensation at the center of public and academic debate. As everyday expenses such as housing and groceries continue to climb, workers across industries have increasingly demanded higher wages to maintain their purchasing power. The postpandemic surge in wage growth is primarily driven by inflation pass-through [@deluca2023postpandemic], meaning rising prices directly translate into upward pressure on salaries. This ongoing tension between inflation and real wages makes compensation trends not just an economic concern, but a lived reality for millions of workers. It is precisely this urgency that motivated us to examine salary and compensation as a research topic. Understanding how and why wages change in response to broader economic pressures is essential for both individuals navigating their careers and organizations designing competitive pay strategies.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What trends make this a crucial area of study in 2024?
text:
What trends make this a crucial area of study in 2024?
Beyond macroeconomic pressures, the explosive growth of artificial intelligence has introduced a new and urgent dimension to compensation research. The demand for AI-related talent has surged dramatically in recent years, creating a structural shift in how employers compete for skilled workers. Research supports the scale of this transformation: Analysis of nearly 15,000 data science salary records from 2020 to 2024 found a consistent upward trend in compensation with 2023 marking the sharpest single-year increase [@bagyam2024datascience], and AI-specialized roles commanding the highest salaries of all job categories. This rapid reshaping of salary structures, driven by AI adoption and talent shortages, makes 2024 a particularly critical moment to study compensation trends. Our project is motivated by the need to capture and understand these shifts as they unfold, providing timely insights into a labor market that is transforming faster than ever before.

title: Research Introduction: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What do you expect to find in your analysis?
text:
What do you expect to find in your analysis?
Based on the analysis of 2021 to 2023 data science compensation [@saeeji2025unveiling], we would expect a 2024 analysis to show continued overall salary growth, with the highest pay concentrated among experienced professionals, full-time employees, contractors in some cases, and senior technical roles, while salary outcomes still vary substantially by company size and location. The article also suggests that higher-paying opportunities are concentrated in select U.S. markets, with Illinois standing out in its sample, and that pay remains uneven overall, with most workers clustered in lower-to-mid salary bands and a smaller group earning very high salaries. This expectation of an uneven wage distribution is further supported by research on AI and wage inequality [@georgieff2024artificial] which highlights how the rapid integration of artificial intelligence tends to widen the salary gap between high-skilled tech roles and traditional occupations. Therefore, in our analysis of the Lightcast dataset, we expect to find a distinct “AI wage premium,” where roles requiring AI competencies significantly outperform non-AI careers in average compensation. Regarding industry-specific impacts, we expect this premium to boost tech, finance, and professional services’ wages the most in 2024. These sectors rapidly adopted AI, so growth will be sharper there. Manufacturing and retail might see slower wage gains. Looking at the analysis of approximately ten million online job vacancies from 2018 to 2024 [@castaneda2026payaiskillsreward], we also expect AI-related careers in 2024 to outperform comparable non-AI roles not only in pay but also in job quality, since AI postings were associated with higher salaries and were significantly more likely to include benefits such as remote work, parental leave, and workplace-culture perks. This article also unveils trends in remote-work by showing that remote options are more common in AI postings than non-AI postings, however, it does not directly establish whether remote jobs pay more than in-office roles overall. By analyzing the Lightcast dataset, we aim to bridge this gap and clarify the intersection of remote work, location, and salary. Both articles and the OECD findings mainly support stronger pay and richer benefits for AI talent and continued location-based pay differences in high-paying U.S. markets which we look forward to validating in the Lightcast jobs dataset.

title: Data Preparation
text:
This page documents the data preparation process used to clean the raw Lightcast job postings dataset. The goal is to create a consistent cleaned dataset for exploratory analysis, salary analysis, regression, classification, and clustering.
The cleaning process includes checking missing values, identifying duplicate job posting IDs, reviewing suspicious salary and date values, cleaning important text and category fields, creating an analysis-ready salary variable, and saving the cleaned dataset for later pages.
Raw file: data/lightcast_job_postings.csv
Output file: data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet



The raw Lightcast dataset contains job posting information such as job titles, companies, locations, industries, occupations, remote-work status, skills, and salary fields. I load the dataset using Spark because the file is large and the later analysis pages also rely on Spark SQL.


Show code
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col,
    count,
    round, 
    percentile_approx,
    trim,
    when,
    isnan,
    to_date,
    regexp_replace,
    sum as spark_sum
)
import os
import pandas as pd

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled

# Start Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

df = spark.read.option("header", "true") \
    .option("inferSchema", "true") \
    .option("multiLine", "true") \
    .option("escape", "\"") \
    .csv("data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")

df.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs")

Salary is a key outcome variable in this project, so I created an ANALYSIS_SALARY field using all available employer-provided salary information. If SALARY was available, it was used directly. If SALARY was missing but both SALARY_FROM and SALARY_TO were available, their midpoint was used. If only one salary bound was available, that value was used. This reduces missing salary values without replacing missing salaries with an overall median.


Show code
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "ANALYSIS_SALARY",
    when(
        col("SALARY").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY") <= 500000),
        col("SALARY")
    ).when(
        col("SALARY").isNull()
        & col("SALARY_FROM").isNotNull()
        & col("SALARY_TO").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_TO") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") <= 500000)
        & (col("SALARY_TO") <= 500000)
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") <= col("SALARY_TO")),
        (col("SALARY_FROM") + col("SALARY_TO")) / 2
    ).when(
        col("SALARY").isNull()
        & col("SALARY_FROM").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_FROM") <= 500000),
        col("SALARY_FROM")
    ).when(
        col("SALARY").isNull()
        & col("SALARY_TO").isNotNull()
        & (col("SALARY_TO") > 0)
        & (col("SALARY_TO") <= 500000),
        col("SALARY_TO")
    ).otherwise(None)
)

This step creates a separate salary variable for sensitivity analysis. Missing salaries are imputed using the median salary within the same industry only when that industry has at least 50% salary availability and at least 30 valid salary observations. The original ANALYSIS_SALARY column is not replaced.


Show code
industry_salary_quality = df_clean.groupBy("NAICS_2022_6_NAME").agg(
    count("*").alias("total_postings"),
    count(
        when(
            (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
            1
        )
    ).alias("salary_available_count"),
    percentile_approx(
        when(
            (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
            col("ANALYSIS_SALARY")
        ),
        0.5
    ).alias("industry_median_salary")
)

industry_salary_quality = industry_salary_quality.withColumn(
    "salary_available_percent",
    round(col("salary_available_count") / col("total_postings") * 100, 2)
)

qualified_industry_medians = industry_salary_quality.filter(
    (col("salary_available_percent") >= 50) &
    (col("salary_available_count") >= 30) &
    (col("industry_median_salary").isNotNull())
).select(
    "NAICS_2022_6_NAME",
    "industry_median_salary",
    "salary_available_count",
    "salary_available_percent"
)

df_clean = df_clean.join(
    qualified_industry_medians,
    on="NAICS_2022_6_NAME",
    how="left"
)

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED",
    when(
        (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
        col("ANALYSIS_SALARY")
    ).when(
        ((col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNull()) | (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") <= 0)) &
        (col("industry_median_salary").isNotNull()),
        col("industry_median_salary")
    ).otherwise(None)
)

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "SALARY_IMPUTATION_STATUS",
    when(
        (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNotNull()) & (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") > 0),
        "Observed salary"
    ).when(
        ((col("ANALYSIS_SALARY").isNull()) | (col("ANALYSIS_SALARY") <= 0)) &
        (col("industry_median_salary").isNotNull()),
        "Imputed using qualified industry median"
    ).otherwise("Still missing")
)

def create_skill_matches(column_name, skill_type):
    return df_clean.select(
        col("ID").alias("job_id"),
        explode(split(col(column_name), ",")).alias("skill_name")
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_name",
        regexp_replace(col("skill_name"), r'[\[\]"]', "")
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_name",
        trim(col("skill_name"))
    ).withColumn(
        "skill_type",
        lit(skill_type)
    ).filter(
        col("skill_name").isNotNull() & (col("skill_name") != "")
    )

specialized_skill_matches = create_skill_matches("SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME", "specialized")
common_skill_matches = create_skill_matches("COMMON_SKILLS_NAME", "common")
software_skill_matches = create_skill_matches("SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME", "software")
certification_matches = create_skill_matches("CERTIFICATIONS_NAME", "certification")

job_skill_matches_raw = specialized_skill_matches.unionByName(
    common_skill_matches
).unionByName(
    software_skill_matches
).unionByName(
    certification_matches
).dropDuplicates(["job_id", "skill_name", "skill_type"])

skills_df = job_skill_matches_raw.select(
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
).dropDuplicates()

skills_df = skills_df.withColumn(
    "skill_id",
    monotonically_increasing_id()
)

skills_df = skills_df.select(
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
)

skills_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skills")

job_skill_matches_df = job_skill_matches_raw.join(
    skills_df,
    on=["skill_name", "skill_type"],
    how="left"
).select(
    "job_id",
    "skill_id",
    "skill_name",
    "skill_type"
)

job_skill_matches_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_skill_matches")

# Create skill-count features for modeling
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum

skill_count_features_df = (
    job_skill_matches_df
    .groupBy("job_id")
    .agg(
        spark_sum(when(col("skill_type") == "common", 1).otherwise(0)).alias("common_skill_count"),
        spark_sum(when(col("skill_type") == "specialized", 1).otherwise(0)).alias("specialized_skill_count"),
        spark_sum(when(col("skill_type") == "software", 1).otherwise(0)).alias("software_skill_count")
    )
)

skill_count_features_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skill_count_features")

# ----------------------------
# AI-related job IDs
# ----------------------------
ai_job_ids = spark.sql("""
SELECT DISTINCT
    j.id AS job_id
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN job_skill_matches s
    ON j.id = s.job_id
WHERE
    LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%generative artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%mlops%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%pytorch%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%artificial neural networks%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%machine learning model training%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%large language modeling%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%random forest algorithm%'
    OR LOWER(s.skill_name) LIKE '%decision tree learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%ai engineer%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%ml engineer%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(j.title_clean) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%machine learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%artificial intelligence%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%generative ai%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%large language model%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%llm%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%deep learning%'
    OR LOWER(j.body) LIKE '%natural language processing%'
""")

ai_job_ids.createOrReplaceTempView("ai_job_ids")


# ----------------------------
# Jobs with AI classification
# ----------------------------
jobs_with_ai = (
    job_postings_df.alias("j")
    .join(
        ai_job_ids.alias("a"),
        col("j.id") == col("a.job_id"),
        "left"
    )
    .withColumn(
        "career_type",
        when(col("a.job_id").isNotNull(), "AI-related")
        .otherwise("Non-AI")
    )
)

jobs_with_ai.createOrReplaceTempView("jobs_with_ai")


# ----------------------------
# Job-level skill flags for modeling
# ----------------------------
skill_features_df = job_skill_matches_df.groupBy("job_id").agg(
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%python%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_python"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%sql%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_sql"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%machine learning%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_machine_learning"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%tableau%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_tableau"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%power bi%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_power_bi"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%amazon web services%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_aws"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%microsoft excel%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_excel"),
    spark_max(when(lower(col("skill_name")).like("%sap%"), 1).otherwise(0)).alias("has_sap")
)

skill_features_df.createOrReplaceTempView("skill_features")


# ----------------------------
# Salary regression-ready dataset
# ----------------------------
salary_regression_data = (
    job_postings_df.alias("j")
    .join(locations_df.alias("l"), col("j.location_id") == col("l.location_id"), "left")
    .join(occupations_df.alias("o"), col("j.occupation_id") == col("o.occupation_id"), "left")
    .join(skill_features_df.alias("s"), col("j.id") == col("s.job_id"), "left")
    .join(ai_job_ids.alias("a"), col("j.id") == col("a.job_id"), "left")
    .select(
        col("j.id"),
        col("j.salary").cast("double").alias("salary"),
        col("l.state_name"),
        col("l.city_name"),
        col("l.msa_name"),
        col("j.title_clean"),
        col("o.specialized_occupation_name"),
        col("o.occupation_group_name"),
        col("j.remote_type_name"),
        col("j.employment_type_name"),
        col("j.min_years_experience"),
        col("j.max_years_experience"),
        when(col("a.job_id").isNotNull(), 1).otherwise(0).alias("is_ai_related"),
        col("s.has_python"),
        col("s.has_sql"),
        col("s.has_machine_learning"),
        col("s.has_tableau"),
        col("s.has_power_bi"),
        col("s.has_aws"),
        col("s.has_excel"),
        col("s.has_sap")
    )
    .filter(
        col("salary").isNotNull() &
        (col("salary") > 0) &
        (col("salary") <= 500000)
    )
    .fillna({
        "state_name": "Unknown",
        "city_name": "Unknown",
        "msa_name": "Unknown",
        "title_clean": "Unknown",
        "specialized_occupation_name": "Unknown",
        "occupation_group_name": "Unknown",
        "remote_type_name": "Unknown",
        "employment_type_name": "Unknown",
        "min_years_experience": 0,
        "max_years_experience": 0,
        "has_python": 0,
        "has_sql": 0,
        "has_machine_learning": 0,
        "has_tableau": 0,
        "has_power_bi": 0,
        "has_aws": 0,
        "has_excel": 0,
        "has_sap": 0
    })
)

salary_regression_data.createOrReplaceTempView("salary_regression_data")


# ----------------------------
# Salary classification-ready dataset
# ----------------------------
avg_salary_value = salary_regression_data.selectExpr(
    "AVG(salary) AS avg_salary"
).collect()[0]["avg_salary"]

salary_classification_data = salary_regression_data.withColumn(
    "above_average_salary",
    when(col("salary") > avg_salary_value, 1).otherwise(0)
)

salary_classification_data.createOrReplaceTempView("salary_classification_data")


# ----------------------------
# Save all prepared tables
# ----------------------------
companies_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/companies.parquet")
industries_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/industries.parquet")
locations_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/locations.parquet")
occupations_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/occupations.parquet")
job_postings_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/job_postings.parquet")
skills_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/skills.parquet")
job_skill_matches_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/job_skill_matches.parquet")
ai_job_ids.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/ai_job_ids.parquet")
jobs_with_ai.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/jobs_with_ai.parquet")
skill_features_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/skill_features.parquet")
salary_regression_data.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/salary_regression_data.parquet")
salary_classification_data.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/salary_classification_data.parquet")
skill_count_features_df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("data/prepared/skill_count_features.parquet")
print("Saved prepared tables to data/prepared/")

title: Data Preparation
text:
This page documents the data preparation process used to clean the raw Lightcast job postings dataset. The goal is to create a consistent cleaned dataset for exploratory analysis, salary analysis, regression, classification, and clustering.
The cleaning process includes checking missing values, identifying duplicate job posting IDs, reviewing suspicious salary and date values, cleaning important text and category fields, creating an analysis-ready salary variable, and saving the cleaned dataset for later pages.
Raw file: data/lightcast_job_postings.csv
Output file: data/cleaned/lightcast_cleaned.parquet

title: Data Preparation
text:
The raw Lightcast dataset contains job posting information such as job titles, companies, locations, industries, occupations, remote-work status, skills, and salary fields. I load the dataset using Spark because the file is large and the later analysis pages also rely on Spark SQL.


Show code
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col,
    count,
    round, 
    percentile_approx,
    trim,
    when,
    isnan,
    to_date,
    regexp_replace,
    sum as spark_sum
)
import os
import pandas as pd

def style_table(df, format_dict=None):
    styled = df.style
    if format_dict is not None:
        styled = styled.format(format_dict)
    styled = styled.set_properties(**{
        "text-align": "left",
        "white-space": "normal",
        "border": "1px solid #ddd",
        "padding": "6px"
    }).set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th", "props": [
            ("background-color", "#f6f8fa"),
            ("color", "#24324b"),
            ("font-weight", "bold"),
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "8px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "td", "props": [
            ("border", "1px solid #ddd"),
            ("padding", "6px")
        ]},
        {"selector": "table", "props": [
            ("border-collapse", "collapse"),
            ("width", "100%")
        ]}
    ])
    return styled

# Start Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost") \
    .appName("SalaryCompensationTrends") \
    .getOrCreate()

spark.catalog.clearCache()

df = spark.read.option("header", "true") \
    .option("inferSchema", "true") \
    .option("multiLine", "true") \
    .option("escape", "\"") \
    .csv("data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")

df.createOrReplaceTempView("raw_jobs")

print("Original rows:", df.count())
print("Original columns:", len(df.columns))

text:
Each job posting should have a unique ID. Duplicate IDs can inflate job counts and bias downstream analysis, so I checked whether any IDs appeared more than once.


Show code

title: Data Preparation
text:
Rows with missing IDs were removed because they cannot be reliably treated as unique job postings. Duplicate IDs were also removed so that each job posting appears only once in the cleaned dataset.


Show code
df_clean_base = df.filter(col("ID").isNotNull())
df_clean_base = df_clean_base.dropDuplicates(["ID"])

print("Rows after removing missing and duplicate IDs:", df_clean_base.count())


Rows after removing missing and duplicate IDs: 72476

title: Data Preparation
text:
Before creating the final cleaned dataset, I reviewed salary and date fields for suspicious values. Salary values were checked for NaN values, non-positive values, unusually high values, invalid salary ranges, and unparseable posting dates.


Show code
df_check = df_clean_base.withColumn("SALARY", col("SALARY").cast("double")) \
                        .withColumn("SALARY_FROM", col("SALARY_FROM").cast("double")) \
                        .withColumn("SALARY_TO", col("SALARY_TO").cast("double"))

suspicious_values = df_check.filter(
    (col("SALARY").isNotNull() & isnan(col("SALARY")))
    | (col("SALARY") <= 0)
    | (col("SALARY") > 500000)
    | (col("SALARY_FROM") < 0)
    | (col("SALARY_TO") < 0)
    | (col("SALARY_FROM") > col("SALARY_TO"))
    | to_date(col("POSTED"), "M/d/yyyy").isNull()
)

print("Suspicious rows after removing missing IDs:", suspicious_values.count())

display(style_table(
    suspicious_values.select(
        "ID",
        "TITLE_CLEAN",
        "COMPANY_NAME",
        "SALARY",
        "SALARY_FROM",
        "SALARY_TO",
        "POSTED",
        "CITY_NAME",
        "STATE_NAME",
        "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"
    ).limit(20).toPandas()
))

checks_pd = pd.DataFrame([
    {"check": "Salary is NaN", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY").isNotNull() & isnan(col("SALARY"))).count()},
    {"check": "Salary <= 0", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY") <= 0).count()},
    {"check": "Salary > 500,000", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY") > 500000).count()},
    {"check": "Salary From < 0", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY_FROM") < 0).count()},
    {"check": "Salary To < 0", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY_TO") < 0).count()},
    {"check": "Salary From > Salary To", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY_FROM") > col("SALARY_TO")).count()},
    {"check": "Unparseable posted date", "count": df_check.filter(to_date(col("POSTED"), "M/d/yyyy").isNull()).count()},
    {"check": "Original SALARY missing", "count": df_check.filter(col("SALARY").isNull()).count()}
])

    "count": "{:,.0f}"
}))
